content.problemStatement:
Chef is making a layered cake consisting of 222 layers.

Chef has NNN options for the first layer, represented by an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​], where AiA_iAi​ represents the size of the iii-th first layer of the cake.

Similarly, Chef has MMM options for the second layer, represented by an array B=[B1,B2,…,BM]B = [B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_M]B=[B1​,B2​,…,BM​], where BiB_iBi​ represents the size of the iii-th second layer of the cake.

Chef must ensure that the size of the second layer is **strictly smaller** than the size of the first layer. How many possible cakes can make by combining one first layer and one second layer, while respecting that constraint?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains 222 integers - NNN and MMM.
The second line contains NNN integers - A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.
The third line contains NNN integers - B1,B2,…,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1​,B2​,…,BM​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of valid layered cakes Chef can make.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : The 222 options are:

Choosing A1=5A_1 = 5A1​=5 and B1=3B_1 = 3B1​=3.
Choosing A3=6A_3 = 6A3​=6 and B1=3B_1 = 3B1​=3.

Test Case 2 : Any of the first layer can be paired with any of the second layers. Thus, there are 3⋅3=93 \cdot 3 = 93⋅3=9 pairs.
